segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Chapter Seven: The Bellwright-Corda Agreement

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father was at the lathe when Cass came in.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The workshop had gone to its night-self: lamplight making the hanging bells into shadows, linseed oil heavier without the forge's competing heat. Eddan worked with his back to the door, hands moving along a clapper shaft, testing the curve. He didn't turn when Cass set his bag on the stone bench.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass came further in and stopped at the edge of the lamp's reach.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I need to ask you something.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The lathe kept turning.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Mm.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The Bellwright-Corda agreement. The one from twelve years ago.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father's hands went still. The lathe slowed. Stopped.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Eddan set the clapper down with the care of a man who needed something to do with his hands, and turned.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: [tense] What about it,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I want to know what it covers.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: The maintenance arrangement. Quarterly reports. Access to the Corda resonance chambers for tuning.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father's voice was flat.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: You've seen the notices.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I've seen the notices. I haven't seen the agreement.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: [hesitantly] It's a standard — it's...

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Standard custodianship. The Corda family holds a — the bronze stock.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A pause.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: We maintain. We file reports.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: What kind of claim?

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: An old one.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father folded the cloth he'd been using. Set it down.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Aldric's son signed a document. Before the tonal system. Written, not sung. The Corda family holds it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Is it enforceable?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Something crossed his father's face, quick and gone.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: It's a historical document. Under current Tonal Law—

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: [firmly] Is it enforceable.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The cooling lathe ticked once.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: No,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: his father said.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: It isn't.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: But she used it anyway.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father looked at him. The lamp threw shadows up under his eyes.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: She raised the question,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Twelve years ago. Whether the Academy should examine the claim. Whether the Bellwright family's custodianship was—

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped. Started again.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: The word she used was

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] fitness.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass's chest went tight.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Fitness,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Whether the Bellwright family was fit to maintain sole custodianship of the forty-seven bells. It would have required a formal review. The Academy examining the journals. The bronze stock records. The casting methods.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He paused.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Everything.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: And Perin signed the service contract instead.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father said nothing.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: That's what it cost,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: To make her not raise the question.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Still nothing. His father had turned slightly, not away but angled, as if the bells on the wall needed looking at.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass watched the back of his head for a moment. Then he said,

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I'm going to the public registry. Tonight. The original filing will be there.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: [worried] Cass—

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I'm not asking for your copy.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He picked up his bag.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I wanted you to know I was going.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He walked out before his father could find the words, if he had any.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The Academy's public registry occupied the ground floor of the oldest building in the district, tight-corridored stone that smelled of chalk and old paper. The registry was open until the evening bell, which gave him forty minutes.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The registrar was a thin man named Adwick who wore copper-colored sleeve guards over his coat. He looked at Cass the way he looked at all Academy students who arrived without a faculty sponsor — measuring.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Bellwright-Corda agreement. Twelve years ago. Custodianship filing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Adwick didn't write anything down. He looked at Cass. His eyes went to the shelves behind him, then back, and something in the look was off — a fraction too long, a fraction too careful.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [tense]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: That's a sealed registry,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Privacy clause, standard for—

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Custodianship agreements are public record. The bells are a civic instrument.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The privacy clause covers the codicil amendments. The primary filing is public.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Adwick paused.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: One moment.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He disappeared into the shelves.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass stood at the counter and pressed his Down the corridor, a student was running scales — descending fifth, over and over, not quite landing the resolution. He made himself stop listening.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Adwick came back with a bound folio, gray-covered, tied with the red cord that marked civic filings. He set it on the counter. Then he set it down again a second time, slightly differently, and said,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: You can read it here. It doesn't leave the room,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: in the tone of a man reciting something he'd been told to say.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I know,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He untied the cord and opened it. Twelve pages, formal Academy script, dense with quarterly maintenance schedules and bronze stock inventories. He read every clause.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He found it on page seven.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He read it once. Read it again.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Co-Custodial Review Clause. In the event that the House of Corda determines, in its sole assessment, that the Bellwright family's continued custodianship is inconsistent with the standards of fitness required by the Academy's Charter of Civic Instruments, the House of Corda reserves the right to petition the Academy for a formal fitness review. Such a petition will require the Bellwright family to submit all records, journals, and documentation pertaining to the original casting and tuning of the forty-seven bells for examination.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: And below it:

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] This co-custodial review right persists for the duration of the Bellwright-Corda service agreement and all amendments thereto, and shall not expire so long as any active service obligation exists between the two parties.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked up.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Adwick was watching him from the far end of the counter. Not working. Watching.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [tense] Cass looked back at the page.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] So long as any active service obligation exists.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin's contract was a service obligation. While Perin was in service, Maret Corda could invoke the fitness review at any moment, for any reason, and the journals opened.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He turned back to page one and looked for the term date.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: There wasn't one. The service obligation was referenced by a codicil number. Sealed. He couldn't see when it ended, or whether it ended, or what conditions would satisfy it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He set his finger on the co-custodial clause and read it a third time.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Sole assessment.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She didn't need a lame chord. She didn't need a provable breach. She needed to say one word to the Academy and the journals opened. And she could say it any time she chose, for as long as Perin's contract ran.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He closed the folio. Retied the cord.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Thank you,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Adwick said,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The codicil records. If you wanted to petition for access—

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I know the process.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Adwick looked at him.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Someone was in yesterday,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Asking about the same filing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass went still.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Who.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Adwick adjusted his sleeve guard.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: I can't say who. I can tell you they weren't a student.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The under-note stirred — not the clean needle-sharp signal of a lie, but something muddier, less certain. A flicker. He'd felt it before in market disputes, when a seller quoted a price they'd already half-convinced themselves was fair. Adwick wasn't lying about someone having been here. That part sat clean in the air between them. But the way he said

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] I can't say

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: — not

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] I don't know,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: not

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] I won't say,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: but

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] I can't

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: — that flickered. The under-note pulsed once behind Cass's left eye, dimmed, pulsed again. As if Adwick had told himself a story about why he couldn't say, and believed the story just enough for the note to lose its edge.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The man across the counter wasn't lying — or he was, but only about which part of the truth was his to give. The important thing rang clean: someone had been here, someone who wasn't a student, someone who knew what to look for. The evasion wrapped around it was something Adwick had convinced himself was duty rather than cowardice, and the conviction was just strong enough to blur the signal.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass kept his voice even.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Did they take anything?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A pause. Long enough to be an answer.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The filing is complete,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Adwick said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The primary filing.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The codicil index,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Is it complete?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Adwick's eyes went to the shelves behind him. Came back. He said,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The codicil index is a separate record,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: in a voice that had gone careful and flat.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Is it complete.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Adwick said nothing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He picked up his bag and walked out.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The evening bell rang as he came through the Academy district gate. B-flat, clean and civic, rolling down from the tower. He stood on the road and let it pass and did not feel the usual thing he felt when the bell sounded right.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Someone had been there yesterday. Before him. Not a student — someone who knew the filing well enough to go straight to the codicil index, and who had enough standing in the registry for Adwick to go careful and flat when asked about it. The tally-song strip gone from Torvald's shelf. Now this. Someone moving ahead of him, taking things off shelves before he arrived.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] Sole assessment.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The clause sat in his chest where a clean chord should have been.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He turned onto the road toward the Merchant Tier.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He didn't make a decision about it. He was already walking.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The Corda compound sat in the inner ring of the bowl, south slope, where the Merchant Tier kept its walled estates and its cedar-scented money. The Corda compound's gate porter had hands that didn't match his position — broad, roughened, the hands of someone who had worked with materials heavier than paper. Cass filed that away without knowing what to do with it. Cass had stood outside the gate before — once, two years ago, on an errand for his father that had required him to deliver a maintenance report to the porter's window. He'd handed over the folio and walked away fast and not looked back. That time he'd had permission to be there. This time he had the co-custodial clause in his bag and his brother's name in his mouth and absolutely nothing else.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He felt the compound's acoustics before he saw the gate.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: It started at the corner of Mercer Lane — a pressure change, subtle, the way your ears register a hillside before your legs do. The Merchant Tier sat close enough to the bowl's center that the amphitheater's geometry was still working, but the Corda compound had its own resonance chambers built into the east wing, and those chambers pushed back against the natural acoustic. Standing at the corner, Cass could feel the interference in his back teeth. A chord that almost resolved, held just short of resolution. The tension of it pressed against the inside of his skull like a thumb against a bruise.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stood there for a moment. Let his jaw loosen. The interference didn't stop, but he stopped fighting it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The gate was iron, tall, the kind designed to look permanent. A porter's window sat to the right. A boy about Cass's age was leaning against the wall beside it, eating something wrapped in paper, not paying attention to anything.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass walked up to the window.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The porter was a broad man with a gray coat and the specific boredom of someone who had been sitting in a small room for a long time. He looked at Cass.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Bellwright,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I'm here about the maintenance schedule.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The porter looked at him.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: You're the Bellwright boy.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: My father sent me.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The words came out steady, which surprised him.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: There's a discrepancy in the quarterly report. The resonance chamber in the east wing — the tuning record doesn't match the inspection notes from last month. He needs to see the chamber logs before he files the correction.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A pause.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He usually comes himself.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: His hands,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: He's been having trouble with the fine work. I've been running the inspections this quarter.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Another pause. The porter looked at him with the expression of a man doing arithmetic he didn't particularly want to do. Cass kept his face neutral and thought about nothing except the specific weight of a pitch-gauge in his hands, the particular angle of a bell inspection, things he actually knew.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Chamber logs are with the house steward,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: the porter said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: East corridor, second door.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The gate opened.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass walked through.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The compound's interior hit him like walking into a different room inside his own head. The interference was worse here — not louder, but denser, layered in a way that suggested the resonance chambers had been running exercises long enough to leave a residue in the air. He could feel the standing waves from two or three different sessions overlapping, none of them fully resolved, all of them pressing. His left hand went flat against his thigh. He kept walking.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The courtyard was stone-flagged and quiet, the afternoon light cutting across it at a low angle. A woman in a dark coat crossed the far end without looking at him. Somewhere in the east wing, someone was running a minor seventh, over and over, the interval climbing and not landing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He knew that exercise.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped walking.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The minor seventh, ascending, no resolution — Perin had drilled it for weeks the summer Cass was six, complaining that it was the most useless interval in the codex because it wanted to resolve so badly and the exercise refused to let it. He'd practiced it in the workshop while their father cast, the reaching note hanging in the air, unfinished, until Cass had started hearing it in his sleep.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Someone in the east wing was running Perin's exercise.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He turned toward the sound.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The east corridor was dim, stone-floored, smelling of the cedar wax used on instrument cases and something underneath it, older — the particular dry smell of a space where sound had been made and held for a long time. The minor seventh came clearer here, closer. Second door, the porter had said. Cass passed the first door without stopping.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped at the second.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Knocked.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The minor seventh cut off.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A pause that had a specific quality — not the pause of someone who hadn't heard, but the pause of someone deciding.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Come in,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: said a voice Cass had not heard in four years, and the under-note was completely, perfectly silent.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin was sitting at a writing desk near the window, a manuscript open in front of him, a pen in his hand. He looked up when Cass came in and his face did several things in quick succession that he then put away, one by one, until what remained was something careful and still.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He was twenty-six. He looked older. He'd grown a beard, short and neat, the kind you grew when you'd stopped thinking about whether you had one. His hands on the desk were the same hands Cass remembered — long-fingered, ink-stained, calloused at the same points from the same work. The room around him was orderly in the way of someone who had learned to keep things orderly because it was the one thing they could control: manuscript stacks squared, pen case closed, a cup of something cold on the corner of the desk.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: How did you get in,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Maintenance schedule discrepancy.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His brother looked at him for a long moment.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: That's father's line.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I know.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: He actually has discrepancies. You just used it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin set the pen down. He didn't stand up. He looked at Cass with an expression that was almost familiar — the dry, sideways look he'd had when Cass was small and had done something inadvisable and Perin was deciding whether to be impressed or alarmed.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: You're going to get thrown out when they check.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Probably.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass came further into the room.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I need to talk to you.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: I can see that.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The co-custodial clause.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Something shifted in Perin's face. Not surprise — something that had been waiting. He said,

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: [gently] Sit down, Cass,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: in a voice that had gone quiet.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass sat in the chair across from the desk. The window behind Perin showed the compound's inner courtyard, a fountain that wasn't running, the east wing's stone face. The minor seventh was gone from the air. In its place, the interference from the resonance chambers pressed on steadily, that unresolved chord sitting in Cass's back teeth like a sliver of metal.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: How long have you known,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: About the clause? Since I signed.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin's hands were flat on the desk.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: I read the whole contract before I ratified. I knew what it was.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: You knew it had no term.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: The codicil was sealed. I didn't know the exact language. But I knew it wasn't—

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped. Looked at the window.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: I knew it wasn't what father told me it was.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: What did he tell you?

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: That it was temporary. That the Corda family needed a composer for a specific project, two or three years, and that the fitness question would go away when the project was complete.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He said it without inflection. Twelve years of saying it to himself.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: I was sixteen. I wanted to believe him.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass looked at his brother's hands on the desk. At the ink stains at the base of his first two fingers.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: You've been composing their contracts,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: For twelve years.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: And she keeps extending.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin didn't answer. That was its own answer.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The interference from the resonance chambers pressed on. Cass's jaw ached with it. He said,

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I went to Torvald Hess. The Compact. I've been learning the tally-song system — the way they document lame chord disputes in the outer districts. Arveth Corda-Laine sang your ratification.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin went still.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: She's on three of Torvald's disputed agreements,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Same structure each time. Minor seventh amendment, no tonic resolution. The codicil floats loose from the original binding because the interval never closed. Torvald's cases are small — two of them settled, one is still pending. But the pattern is the same.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He heard himself speaking faster and made himself slow down.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: If your standing-wave record shows the same amendment frequency—

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Cass.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: —that's demonstrable grounds. The Academy keeps acoustic records separate from the contract terms, they don't fall under the privacy clause, and if Arveth Corda-Laine left the amendment interval unresolved across multiple—

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Cass.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin's voice was quiet. Not sharp, not tired. Just quiet, in a way that stopped him.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: The standing-wave record from my ratification is gone. I looked for it three years ago. Someone filed a correction notice — clerical error, standard form — and the original record was pulled.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Cass felt something drop out of his chest.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Adwick,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The registrar. Someone was there yesterday, before me. Not a student.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: How do you know that?

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: He told me. He was—

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: He was trying to warn me, I think. He couldn't say it directly. But he told me the codicil index was touched.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin was quiet for a moment.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: [sad] They've been thorough,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: They've had twelve years to be thorough.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: [firmly] So we find another angle.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass heard the edge in his own voice and didn't try to smooth it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The co-custodial clause itself — the fact that it's structured so the fitness review can never expire as long as you're in service. That's not a standard clause. Torvald knows a Court Singer, Dav Sorn, who's independent of Corda. If Sorn would examine the original agreement—

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: And say what? That the clause is aggressive?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin's voice was still quiet. That was almost worse than if he'd sounded angry.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Aggressive isn't void, Cass. The Academy will look at the interval, confirm the ratification was in proper form, and tell you the terms are the terms.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The ratification had a lame chord.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: That you can't prove.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The codicil index was pulled. That's evidence of—

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: That's evidence someone filed a correction notice.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin picked up the pen and set it down again. The same gesture their father made with the folding cloth.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: I've been through this. I've been through every version of this for twelve years.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The room was very quiet. Outside, the fountain didn't run. The interference from the resonance chambers sat in Cass's teeth.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Then show me what you have,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin looked at him.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: You've been through every version for twelve years,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Show me what you found. Show me what's left.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A long pause. Perin's eyes went to the manuscript on the desk, and then to Cass, and then to something past Cass's shoulder that wasn't in the room. Then he opened the desk drawer and took out a bundle of letters, tied with a cord, and set them on the desk between them.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass recognized the handwriting on the top one. His father's.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: He writes every month,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin said.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Has since the first year.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: He told me you write. He said—

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass stopped.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] Your brother is well, he writes.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: He never said he wrote to you.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: No. He wouldn't.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin untied the cord. The letters fanned slightly, a thick stack, twelve years of monthly correspondence.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Read the third one from the top.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass picked it up. Opened it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father's handwriting, cramped and careful. A date four months after Perin's signing. Three paragraphs about the workshop — a new clapper cast for the Seventh of Ash Street, a problem with the bronze stock, the quarterly report filed on time. Then, at the bottom, in smaller script, as if added after:

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] I have read the journals. All of them. I want you to know that what I did, I did knowing what was in them, and I did it anyway, and I have not found a way to say that to you that doesn't sound like an excuse. It isn't an excuse. I am telling you so that you know I understood the cost.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass read it twice. Set it down.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: He knows what's in the journals,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: What's in them.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Something Aldric put into the first bells. Something in the sequence — the Civic Pitch, the cumulative harmonic. It's not in any codex.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He paused.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Have you been hearing something in the morning pitch? Something low. Underneath.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] The question arrived like a bell struck in a quiet room.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin nodded slowly, as if something had been confirmed that he'd been carrying for a long time.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: I thought you might. You always heard more than you should.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at the letters.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: It's not a binding. It's not a command. Aldric composed it as — Cass, I've been trying to find the right word for four years. The closest I have is

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] evidence.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: He composed evidence into the bells. Of what the system was built to do and what it was built to hide.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His jaw was tight. He hadn't noticed when it had started.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The fitness review,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The journals. That's what Maret Corda is actually holding. Not just the bronze stock claim. She knows what's in them.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: She's known for thirty years, from what I can piece together. She built the whole Corda empire partly around the fact that the Bellwright family would do anything to keep those journals closed.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: [tense] Including give her you.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin said nothing. He reached out and retied the cord around the letters, neat and precise.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Read the intervals,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: What?

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: In the letters. The technical observations — the chamber tunings, the maintenance notes, the things I describe. I've been writing to you since you were old enough to read them. Not the letters you've seen.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at Cass.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: The ones I've been putting inside the letters you've seen.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass stared at him.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: The specific intervals I mention. The exact frequencies. The sequence they appear in.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin's voice was still quiet, but something had shifted underneath it — not hope exactly, but the thing that lives in the same house as hope.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: I've been building a record. Every lame chord I've composed for them, every amendment that didn't resolve, every codicil that floated loose — I've been encoding the pattern in the maintenance notes. The observations are real. The sequence they appear in is a message.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: To me.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: I didn't know when you'd be old enough to read it. I didn't know if you'd hear what I put in it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at Cass steadily.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: But you went to Torvald Hess. You learned the tally-song system. You found Arveth Corda-Laine across three disputed agreements.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He paused.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Read them again.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass looked at the bundle of letters on the desk between them.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Twelve years of monthly correspondence. His father's cramped handwriting, the small script at the bottom of the third one from the top.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] I have read the journals. I want you to know I understood the cost.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: And inside those, something else. A record Perin had been building in the gaps, in the technical observations, in the specific intervals he described — a message written in the only language Maret Corda's people wouldn't think to read, because it was too small and too patient and it had been waiting for someone who could hear what the intervals said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Outside, the fountain didn't run. The resonance chambers pressed on with their unresolved chord, reaching and reaching and not landing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass picked up the bundle.

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Read them again,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin said.
